title: Fabricated Components, In-Service Materials, Coatings, and Other Applications
narrationScript: In Level One you learned to see. In Level Two you are expected to know the hardware. This lesson walks through the components you will actually inspect, what happens to materials in service, what coatings are telling you, and where your authority to make calls comes from. Start with pressure vessels. The visual priorities are the welded seams, the nozzles, and the attachments. Longitudinal seams are staggered from course to course, so use the drawing to find where each one runs before you climb. At nozzles, inspect the nozzle to shell weld and the reinforcing pad welds, and confirm the little weep hole in the pad is open. That hole exists so a leak past the inner weld can show itself. If somebody painted it shut, write it up. Check manway gasket faces for scoring and distortion, and check support and lug welds, because attachment welds carry local stresses the shell does not. Piping adds motion and flow. Walk the system. Hangers and supports should carry load the way the design intended. A spring can bottomed out, a shoe lifted off its steel, a missing nut on a U bolt, these are visual findings that matter. Erosion concentrates where flow changes direction or speeds up, so give extra time to elbows, reducers, and anything downstream of a control valve or orifice. Corrosion under insulation hides at damaged jacketing, at penetrations, and at low points. You will not see the corrosion through the insulation. You see the conditions that let water in, and you target those spots for closer work. Rotating and pressure controlling equipment gives you short windows during overhaul. On pumps, look at the impeller. Cavitation leaves sharp edged, sponge like pitting near the suction eye and on the low pressure side of the vanes. Check wear rings for rubbing and galling, and shaft sleeves for scoring. On valves, the classic finding is wire drawing, fine erosion channels cut across a seat by high velocity leakage when the valve throttles nearly closed. Fasteners deserve respect too. Verify grade markings against the documents, check thread engagement, and look for necking, corrosion, galling, and damaged threads. In service, materials degrade in patterns you can learn. Adhesive wear smears and transfers metal. Abrasive wear scratches and gouges. Fretting happens at clamped joints that vibrate, and on steel it leaves a fine reddish brown powder. Corrosion has a vocabulary of its own. Uniform attack thins everything evenly. Pitting digs cavities whose depth can exceed their width, which is why a pit gauge matters. Crevice corrosion hides under gaskets and deposits. Galvanic attack eats the less noble metal near a junction of dissimilar metals. Intergranular attack gives a sugary surface near welds in sensitized stainless steel. Stress corrosion cracking shows tight, fine, branched cracks, and they are easy to miss without magnification. That is where field microscopy earns its place. Portable microscopes and measuring magnifiers in the range of ten to one hundred power let you separate a crack from a scratch, and replication techniques record the microstructure on site without cutting the part. Coatings are part of visual testing too. On paint systems you verify surface preparation before the first coat goes down, you check each coat for contamination and pinholes, and you inspect the final film for runs, sags, orange peel, blisters, peeling, and holidays, which are breaks that expose the substrate. Zinc coatings and zinc rich primers protect steel sacrificially, so bare areas and white storage stain matter. Conversion coatings like phosphate and chromate read mostly by color. Uniform color and complete coverage are the visual cues. Anodized aluminum should show even color and sheen with no thin spots and no crazing. Beyond metals, ceramics show chips, edge flakes, and crazing of the glaze. Composites hide damage. An impact that barely dents the paint can mean delamination underneath, so barely visible impact damage gets flagged for follow up. Plastics craze and stress whiten. Bearings tell their stories at the raceways. Spalling from fatigue, true brinelling from static overload, false brinelling from vibration while the machine sat parked. Finally, remember the frame around all of it. Codes decide when visual testing is required, how much of it, and what acceptance criteria apply. Your employer's written practice, built on industry guidance, or the aerospace standard when that applies, decides what you personally are certified to do. Level Two means you perform, interpret, evaluate, and report. Stay inside that, and put your findings in writing every time.

sections.heading: Pressure Vessels and Piping Systems
sections.body:
On **pressure vessels**, VT concentrates where stress and fabrication meet: longitudinal and circumferential shell seams (long seams are staggered between courses — locate them from the drawing before you climb), nozzle and reinforcing-pad welds, manway gasket faces, and attachment welds for supports, lugs, and ladders. Verify that repad **weep holes** are open, not painted or puttied shut — they exist so leakage past the inner weld can reveal itself. Inside a vessel during an outage, look for bulging and distortion, weld-metal wastage, grooving at liquid-level lines, and damage to cladding or linings. New-construction VT verifies fit-up, weld profiles, and freedom from arc strikes; in-service VT hunts service damage against the baseline.

**Piping systems** add geometry and motion. Walk the line. Check **supports and hangers**: spring-can indicators within their travel range, shoes still in contact with their steel, no bottomed-out or fully lifted hangers, no missing U-bolt nuts. Erosion concentrates where flow changes direction or velocity — elbow outer radius, downstream of orifices and control valves, at reducers. **Corrosion under insulation (CUI)** develops where water gets in: damaged jacketing, failed sealant at penetrations, low points, and saddle contact areas — prioritize those locations for stripping or inspection ports. At flanges, look for leakage staining, gasket extrusion, and proper fastener engagement.

sections.caption: Priority visual examination zones on a horizontal pressure vessel, from staggered long seams to repad weep holes and saddle attachment welds.

sections.heading: Pumps, Valves, and Fasteners
sections.body:
**Pumps** opened for overhaul give VT a short window — use it systematically. Inspect impeller vanes for **cavitation damage**: sharp-edged, sponge-like pitting concentrated near the suction eye and on the low-pressure face of the vanes. Check wear-ring surfaces for rubbing and galling, shaft sleeves for scoring at packing or seal contact, and the casing for erosion channels, especially near the cutwater.

**Valves**: seats and discs suffer **wire drawing** — fine erosion channels cut across the seating surface by high-velocity leakage when a valve throttles nearly closed. Stems show scoring and bent-stem wobble; bodies erode at the seat throat. After lapping or repair, confirm the seating band is continuous and damage-free all the way around.

**Fasteners** are a VT staple on every fabricated assembly. Verify the **grade or head markings** match the bill of materials, check thread engagement against the governing spec (many require the bolt end at least flush with, or protruding past, the nut), and look for necking or stretch, corrosion, thread damage, galling on stainless threads, missing washers, and mixed materials that invite galvanic attack. A cheap bolt in the wrong place fails like an expensive one.

sections.heading: In-Service Degradation: Wear, Corrosion/Erosion, and Field Microscopy
sections.body:
Wear leaves fingerprints. **Adhesive wear** (galling, seizing) smears and transfers metal between sliding surfaces. **Abrasive wear** scratches and gouges in the direction of motion. **Fretting** attacks clamped joints under vibration, leaving fine oxide debris — reddish-brown 'cocoa' on steel — at faying surfaces and fastener seats.

Corrosion forms you must recognize on sight:

- **Uniform** — even thinning under general rust or scale.
- **Pitting** — small mouth, deep cavity; depth can exceed width, so measure with a pit gauge, never by eye.
- **Crevice** — under gaskets, deposits, and lap joints where stagnant chemistry develops.
- **Galvanic** — attack of the less noble metal concentrated near a dissimilar-metal junction.
- **Intergranular** — a sugary, granular surface, often adjacent to welds in sensitized stainless.
- **Stress-corrosion cracking (SCC)** — tight, fine, branched cracks needing tensile stress plus a specific environment.
- **Erosion-corrosion** — smooth, directional grooves and horseshoe-shaped pits following the flow.

When an indication is too fine to call, **field microscopy** earns its place: portable microscopes and measuring magnifiers (roughly 10–100×) separate cracks from scratches, and in-situ **metallographic replication** records microstructure — creep cavitation, for instance — without cutting the component. Record the magnification used in the report.

sections.caption: Cross-section morphologies of common in-service damage: pitting, uniform thinning, erosion at an elbow, and branched stress-corrosion cracking.

sections.heading: Coatings: Paint, Conversion Coatings, and Anodizing
sections.body:
Coating inspection is staged VT. **Before painting**, verify surface preparation — cleanliness and blast profile compared against the written and photographic standards the procedure invokes. **Between coats**, look for contamination, overspray, and pinholes. **On the final film**, the classic visual defects are runs and sags, orange peel, blistering, peeling, rust bleed-through, and **holidays** — breaks or misses that expose the substrate. VT confirms continuity and workmanship; dry-film-thickness gauges confirm thickness — the two checks travel together.

**Cathodic protection by coating**: zinc — galvanizing or zinc-rich primer — corrodes sacrificially to protect the steel beneath. Visual targets are bare or damaged areas, white storage stain, and coating loss at edges and welds. **Conversion coatings** (phosphate, chromate) are thin chemical films read mostly by color: uniform color, complete coverage, and no loose powdery residue.

**Anodizing** grows a hard aluminum-oxide layer electrolytically. Look for even color and sheen, complete coverage at edges and holes, no crazing, and no contact marks from racking. Color or sheen variation flags thin or unsealed film and gets verified by the lab — your job is to catch it.

sections.caption: A protective coating system in cross-section, with the visual defects — holiday, blister, run, and pinhole — that VT must catch at each stage.

sections.heading: Ceramics, Composites, Plastics, and Bearings
sections.body:
**Ceramics** are brittle and unforgiving: look for chips, edge flakes, cracks, and **crazing** — a fine network of surface cracks in glazed parts. Strong raking light and a high-contrast background do most of the work; thermal-shock cracks favor section changes.

**Composites** hide their damage. An impact that barely marks the paint — **BVID, barely visible impact damage** — can mean delamination and fiber breakage underneath, so a slight dent, scuff, or fiber shadow gets marked and referred for follow-up evaluation, not waved through. Also look for delamination at edges and fastener holes, resin-starved areas with a dry-fiber sheen, resin-rich pockets, blisters, and heat scorch.

**Plastics** show **crazing** under load and solvent exposure, stress-whitening at bends, sink marks and knit (weld) lines from molding, and chalking from UV exposure.

**Bearings** tell their history at the raceways: **spalling** (fatigue flaking), **true brinelling** (dents at rolling-element spacing from static overload), **false brinelling** (fretting wear at the same spacing from vibration while stationary), cage wear, scoring from contamination, and overheating discoloration running straw to blue. The spacing pattern is the giveaway — measure it against the rolling-element pitch.

sections.title: Codes and Certification Set the Frame
sections.body: Codes and specifications — construction codes for vessels and piping, structural welding codes, in-service inspection codes — define **when VT is required, how much, and against what acceptance criteria**. Your authority to perform and evaluate comes from your employer's certification program: a written practice following industry guidance such as SNT-TC-1A, or NAS410 in aerospace, with documented training, experience, examinations, and current vision tests. A Level II performs the examination, interprets and evaluates against the criteria the code names, and signs the record — within the limits of that certification, and no further.

figures.title: Coating System Cross-Section and Common Defects

checkQuestions.question: Sharp-edged, sponge-like pitting concentrated near a centrifugal pump impeller's suction eye most likely indicates:
checkQuestions.explanation: Cavitation bubbles form where local pressure is lowest — near the suction eye and the low-pressure vane faces — and collapse as pressure recovers, hammering the surface into characteristic sharp, sponge-like pitting.

checkQuestions.question: Where should corrosion-under-insulation (CUI) inspection be prioritized on an insulated line?
options: Jacket damage, penetrations, failed sealant, and low points
checkQuestions.explanation: CUI needs water under the insulation, so the priority locations are wherever water can enter and collect — damaged jacketing, penetrations, failed seals, saddles, and low points.

checkQuestions.question: Fine erosion channels cut across a valve seat by high-velocity leakage while the valve throttled nearly closed are called:
checkQuestions.explanation: Wire drawing is the classic valve-seat finding: thin, wire-like erosion channels carved by high-velocity fluid squeezing across the seating surface.

checkQuestions.question: Indentation-like marks at rolling-element spacing in a bearing raceway, formed by vibration while the shaft was NOT rotating, are:
checkQuestions.explanation: False brinelling is fretting wear at the element spacing produced by small oscillations in a stationary bearing; true brinelling is plastic denting from static overload.

checkQuestions.question: Which document tailors industry recommendations into your employer's specific VT qualification and certification requirements?
checkQuestions.explanation: Guidance such as SNT-TC-1A is a recommended practice; the employer's written practice converts it into the binding requirements — training hours, experience, exams, vision tests — for that employer's personnel.

references: CP-105-2024 VT Topical Outline, Level II Supplement §3.5–3.9
Pressure vessel and piping codes requiring visual examination (generic, e.g., ASME Section V Article 9, in-service inspection codes)
SNT-TC-1A and NAS410 personnel qualification frameworks (generic reference)
